title: SEGMENT 1 — The hidden world, and why we couldn't see it

lines.text: SEGMENT 1 — The hidden world, and why we couldn't see it
Alright, let's get started.
Good morning, everyone.

Today and for the next two hours we're going to talk about the microbiome — the staggeringly large and largely invisible world of microbes that live on you, in you, in the soil under your feet, in the ocean, in the deep subsurface, basically everywhere there is liquid water and a little bit of energy.
And we're going to talk about the technology that finally let us see it: metagenomics.
Culture-free DNA sequencing of entire microbial communities.
The single most important methodological revolution in microbiology since Pasteur and Koch built the foundations of the field in the late 1800s.
I want to start with a number, because the numbers in this field are genuinely staggering and you should feel a little disoriented by them.
Your body, by cell count, is roughly half microbial.

The old number you might have heard was ten to one — ten bacterial cells for every human cell — and a careful 2016 estimate by Ron Sender, Shai Fuchs, and Ron Milo at the Weizmann Institute revised that downward to closer to one to one.
So somewhere around thirty trillion of your own cells, somewhere around thirty-eight trillion bacterial cells.
By count, you are about half microbe.

By gene content, you are overwhelmingly microbe — your own genome encodes about twenty thousand protein-coding genes, and the collective genome of your gut bacteria encodes somewhere between two and twenty million.
That's a hundred to a thousand times more genetic potential sitting in your colon than in all of your own chromosomes combined.

And that potential is doing chemistry — digesting fibers you can't digest, synthesizing vitamins you can't make, training your immune system, producing neurotransmitters that talk to your brain, defending the gut against invading pathogens — chemistry you, the host, cannot do on your own.
Here is the analogy I want you to hold for the next two hours.
You are a trillion-microbe ecosystem walking around in a coat of skin.
You're not a single organism.
You're a holobiont — a host plus its associated microbial communities — and the microbial part outnumbers the host part by cell, by gene, and by metabolic capacity.
The lichen analogy is sometimes used: a lichen is a fungus and an alga so deeply intertwined that the combination behaves as a third thing.
You are something similar, just much larger and with much more complicated bacteria.
The fact that we used to think of ourselves as essentially sterile, with maybe a few germs floating around that occasionally caused infections, is not a small correction.
It is a complete reframing of what an animal is.
So how could we have missed this for so long?

How is it that bacteria were discovered by Leeuwenhoek in the 1670s — he literally looked at scrapings from his own teeth under his homemade microscope and was astonished to see tiny "animalcules" swimming around — and yet for the next three hundred years, microbiology remained largely blind to the diversity of microbial life?
The answer is a single technical limitation, and it is the central frame of this whole lecture, so I want to plant it deep right now.
Microbiology, from Koch's postulates onward, was built on the ability to grow microbes in pure culture.
You take a sample, you streak it on an agar plate, you isolate a single colony, you grow it up, you study it.
That's how Koch identified the anthrax bacillus, the tuberculosis bacillus, the cholera bacillus.
The pure-culture method is the workhorse of classical microbiology, and it produced essentially everything we knew about bacteria for a century.
The problem is that the overwhelming majority of microbes on Earth do not grow in pure culture in the lab.
They won't form a colony on agar.
They need partners, or strange nutrients, or oxygen tensions, or temperatures, or chemical conditions that nobody has figured out how to replicate.
Estimates vary by environment, but a useful rough number is that fewer than one percent of the bacterial species in a typical soil or marine sample have ever been cultured.
Ninety-nine percent of them, when you put them on a Petri dish, just sit there and do nothing, or die, or refuse to form a visible colony.

This was sometimes called the great plate count anomaly — the observation, made repeatedly through the twentieth century, that if you count bacterial cells in a soil sample under a microscope you get one number, and if you count colonies that grow from the same sample on a plate you get a number a hundred or a thousand times smaller.
There were vastly more bacteria in the sample than the agar plate could grow.
So picture the situation a microbiologist was in around 1980.
You knew there were lots of bacteria out there.
You could see them down a microscope.
You could weigh them in a soil sample.
But you couldn't grow most of them, and if you couldn't grow them, you couldn't get enough DNA to sequence, you couldn't identify them, you couldn't characterize what they did.
The unculturable majority was a vast dark continent, visible at low resolution but impenetrable to the tools of the field.
You'd been looking at biology with one eye closed.

The eye that was open — the culturable minority, mostly fast-growing organisms that thrive on simple sugars in standard laboratory conditions — gave you a deeply skewed picture of what microbial life actually was.
It was as if a zoologist had tried to catalog all animal life by looking only at pigeons and rats, because those were the only species that would live in his lab.
The thing that opened the second eye was DNA sequencing.

Not the sequencing of whole genomes — that came later, and we'll get there — but the much narrower and more profound trick of pulling DNA directly out of an environmental sample, without culturing anything at all, and reading just enough of the right gene to identify what was there.
That trick was the brainchild of a Berkeley microbiologist named Norman Pace in 1985, and we'll meet him properly in Segment 3.

And once the trick existed, and once sequencing got cheap enough to do it at scale — which happened around 2005 to 2010, with the rise of next-generation sequencing platforms we covered in another lecture — the field exploded.
The Human Microbiome Project launched in 2007.
The American Gut Project started in 2012.
By the late 2010s, every body site, every soil sample, every ocean transect was being characterized by direct DNA sequencing.
The hidden world became visible.

If you walk out of here in two hours and you can do three things — one, sketch the difference between 16S rRNA surveys and shotgun metagenomics and explain when you'd choose each; two, explain what a MAG, a metagenome-assembled genome, is and why it matters; three, read any modern microbiome paper that says "we performed 16S sequencing" or "we did shotgun metagenomics" and have a real intuition for what the data looks like, what the analysis pipeline did, and what the conclusions can and cannot
support — then we've succeeded.
Everything else hangs off those three.
One last orienting note before we dive into the history.
The microbiome field is, in 2026, somewhere between the wild-frontier phase and the mature-clinical phase.
We have one therapy — fecal microbiota transplant for recurrent Clostridioides difficile infection — that genuinely works and is now FDA-approved in defined formulations.
We have an enormous body of correlative data linking gut microbiome composition to inflammatory bowel disease, obesity, depression, autism, allergies, autoimmunity, and cancer immunotherapy response.
And we have a small but growing number of engineered microbiome therapeutics in clinical trials.

We also have an enormous amount of overhyped journalism, weak associations passed off as causation, probiotic products that probably do nothing, and a real risk that the field will overpromise and underdeliver.
Part of my job today is to give you the tools to tell the strong science from the weak.
We'll come back to that explicitly in Segment 10.
Now let's go meet the people who built the field.
